topic.topic: What causes skill-library bloat in lifelong-learning agents?

topic_summary: co_scientist research package for: What causes skill-library bloat in lifelong-learning agents?

primitive_definitions.name: Skill
primitive_definitions.definition: A reusable policy or option that maps states to actions, stored in a library for lifelong learning.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: The core unit of analysis; skill-library bloat is defined by the number and redundancy of skills.

primitive_definitions.name: Task boundary
primitive_definitions.definition: The transition point between distinct tasks in a lifelong learning sequence, characterized by changes in goal, environment, or reward function.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: Task granularity is manipulated by defining the similarity between consecutive tasks; over-specialization is hypothesized to occur at fine boundaries.

primitive_definitions.name: Generalization
primitive_definitions.definition: The ability to apply a learned skill to a task different from the one in which it was acquired, measured by transfer performance.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: Failure to generalize across similar tasks is the proposed cause of skill redundancy.

assumptions.assumption: Agents store all discovered skills in an unbounded library with no compression or pruning.
assumptions.rationale: To isolate the effect of task granularity, we assume no external constraints on library size; bloat is defined as growth beyond what is functionally necessary.
assumptions.possible_failure: If the agent employs implicit compression (e.g., shared parameters across skills), the hypothesis may not hold.

assumptions.assumption: Skill similarity can be reliably quantified using either behavioral or embedding metrics.
assumptions.rationale: To measure redundancy and determine if skills are near-identical, a similarity metric must be defined and validated.
assumptions.possible_failure: If similarity metrics are noisy or fail to capture functional equivalence, results may be confounded.

variables.name: topic condition
variables.description: The main condition or task distribution studied by the baseline.

variables.name: proposed mechanism
variables.description: Lifelong-learning agents with fine-grained task boundaries create highly specialized skills for each task variant, failing to recognize and reuse commonalities across similar tasks. This leads to the storage of many near-identical skills, inflating the library without proportional functional benefit.

variables.name: research quality
variables.description: Whether the proposed question is falsifiable and experimentable.

causal_chain.step: Lifelong-learning agents with fine-grained task boundaries create highly specialized skills for each task variant, failing to recognize and reuse commonalities across similar tasks. This leads to the storage of many near-identical skills, inflating the library without proportional functional benefit.

mechanism_model.mechanism_summary: Lifelong-learning agents with fine-grained task boundaries create highly specialized skills for each task variant, failing to recognize and reuse commonalities across similar tasks. This leads to the storage of many near-identical skills, inflating the library without proportional functional benefit.

tensions.statement: Maximizing task performance through specialization on fine-grained tasks paradoxically leads to inefficient skill libraries, as the cost of storage and retrieval may outweigh the marginal performance gains.
tensions.why_it_matters: Existing lifelong learning research focuses on catastrophic forgetting and performance retention, not on the growth of skill libraries as a function of task granularity. This work isolates the effect of task decomposition on library redundancy, providing a new perspective on inefficiency in continual learning systems.

candidate_questions.question: Does over-specialization due to fine-grained task boundaries cause skill-library bloat in lifelong-learning agents?
candidate_questions.expected_contribution: Existing lifelong learning research focuses on catastrophic forgetting and performance retention, not on the growth of skill libraries as a function of task granularity. This work isolates the effect of task decomposition on library redundancy, providing a new perspective on inefficiency in continual learning systems.

tension_or_contradiction.why_it_matters: Existing lifelong learning research focuses on catastrophic forgetting and performance retention, not on the growth of skill libraries as a function of task granularity. This work isolates the effect of task decomposition on library redundancy, providing a new perspective on inefficiency in continual learning systems.

research_question.question: Does over-specialization due to fine-grained task boundaries cause skill-library bloat in lifelong-learning agents?
research_question.expected_contribution: Existing lifelong learning research focuses on catastrophic forgetting and performance retention, not on the growth of skill libraries as a function of task granularity. This work isolates the effect of task decomposition on library redundancy, providing a new perspective on inefficiency in continual learning systems.

hypothesis.statement: Skill-library bloat is caused by agents over-specializing to fine-grained task variants, which prevents them from reusing skills across similar tasks, leading to storage of many near-identical skills.
hypothesis.predicted_effect: As task granularity increases (i.e., tasks become more similar), the number of skills stored grows superlinearly relative to the number of tasks, and the average pairwise similarity among skills increases, indicating redundancy.

minimal_decisive_test.experiment: Train a hierarchical lifelong-learning agent with skill discovery (e.g., using options or neural skills) on sequences of tasks that vary in granularity. In the fine-grained condition, tasks are slight variations of a base task (e.g., navigate to different goal locations in the same maze). In the coarse-grained condition, tasks are diverse (e.g., different mazes with different dynamics). After each task, record the number of skills in the library and compute pairwise skill similarity using behavioral cloning or embedding cosine similarity. Run multiple random seeds and compare library size and redundancy between conditions.
dependent_variables: number of skills stored in library
average pairwise skill similarity (cosine or behavioral distance)
controls: same agent architecture and skill discovery algorithm
same total number of tasks across conditions
minimal_decisive_test.falsifying_observation: If the library size per task is not significantly larger in the fine-grained condition compared to the coarse-grained condition (e.g., ratio of skills to tasks is approximately equal across conditions), the hypothesis is falsified. Specifically, a t-test failing to reject the null (p >= 0.05) would indicate no causal effect of task granularity on bloat.

supports_hypothesis: Fine-grained condition yields significantly larger library (p < 0.05) with high redundancy (average similarity > 0.8).
Coarse-grained condition yields smaller library with lower redundancy (average similarity < 0.5).
Number of skills grows sublinearly with tasks in coarse condition but superlinearly in fine condition.
rejects_hypothesis: Library sizes are comparable across conditions.
Skill similarity is not higher in fine-grained condition.
Growth rates are similar (e.g., both linear).

failure_update_rule.if_failed: If hypothesis is rejected, we will systematically test alternative causes: first, add a regularization penalty (H002) to see if that mitigates bloat; second, stabilize representations (H003) to check for drift. If neither works, consider that bloat may be a natural consequence of exploration or that skill libraries are inherently redundant. The final question may shift to identifying the primary driver among multiple factors.
failure_update_rule.assumption_to_revise: Agents store all discovered skills in an unbounded library with no compression or pruning.
failure_update_rule.next_question: What mechanism explains failure of: Does over-specialization due to fine-grained task boundaries cause skill-library bloat in lifelong-learning agents?

hypotheses.question: Does over-specialization due to fine-grained task boundaries cause skill-library bloat?
hypotheses.hypothesis: Skill-library bloat arises because agents create highly specific skills for each task variant, failing to generalize commonalities across similar tasks.
hypotheses.mechanism: The agent's skill discovery algorithm treats each episode or task instance as distinct, leading to storage of near-identical skills that differ only in minor details, thus inflating the library.
hypotheses.experiment: Train a lifelong-learning agent on a sequence of tasks that are incremental variations of a base task (e.g., same navigation environment with different obstacle placements). Compare the number of skills stored against an agent trained on tasks with high diversity (e.g., different environments). Measure skill overlap using similarity metrics.
hypotheses.novelty_rationale: Existing work on lifelong learning focuses on catastrophic forgetting or performance retention, not on the growth of skill libraries due to over-specialization. This hypothesis isolates the effect of task granularity on library size.
risks: High computational cost for training multiple agents with different task sequences.
Difficulty in controlling task similarity precisely.
Potential confounding factors like exploration strategy differences.

hypotheses.question: Does the absence of a regularization penalty on library size cause unbounded skill accumulation?
hypotheses.hypothesis: Without a cost for adding new skills, agents accumulate skills indefinitely because the objective function only rewards task performance, not parsimony, leading to skill-library bloat.
hypotheses.mechanism: The learning objective exclusively targets task reward, ignoring the computational or memory cost of storing skills. Thus, adding any skill that offers marginal performance gain is favored, even if it duplicates existing skills.
hypotheses.experiment: Train a baseline agent with standard reward maximization. Train a second agent with a modified objective that includes a small negative penalty proportional to the number of skills in the library. Compare library size and task performance after a fixed number of tasks. Assess whether the penalty reduces bloat without harming performance.
hypotheses.novelty_rationale: Prior work on skill discovery often assumes fixed capacity or manually tuned pruning. This hypothesis explicitly tests the effect of regularization on library growth in a lifelong setting.
risks: The penalty might overly restrict useful skill acquisition, hurting performance.
Difficulty in setting the penalty coefficient appropriately.
May not generalize across different skill representation schemes.

hypotheses.question: Does skill redundancy caused by incompatible state representations lead to library bloat?
hypotheses.hypothesis: When the agent's low-level state representations shift over time (e.g., due to non-stationary environments or representation learning updates), skills learned in one context fail to transfer to new contexts, forcing the agent to learn redundant skills.
hypotheses.mechanism: Representation drift reduces the similarity between skill embeddings across different time steps, causing the skill-matching threshold to not be met even for functionally equivalent skills. This leads to duplicate entries in the library.
hypotheses.experiment: Train an agent with a standard representation learning update (e.g., an autoencoder) and another agent with a representation alignment loss that minimizes drift across time (e.g., using contrastive learning on past states). Compare the size of the skill library after a sequence of tasks. Also evaluate the overlap of skills using a behavioral similarity metric.
hypotheses.novelty_rationale: Existing research on representation learning for reinforcement learning focuses on sample efficiency or generalization, not on its impact on skill library growth. This hypothesis connects representation stability to skill bloat.
risks: Implementing a proper alignment loss may be complex and require careful tuning.
Representation drift might not be the primary cause of bloat in all settings.
The experiment's outcome may depend heavily on the chosen similarity metric for skills.

critiques: H001 (Over-specialization) is novel in isolating task granularity as a cause, mechanism clear, experiment feasible with controlled task similarity; risk of computational cost but manageable.
H002 (Regularization penalty) is straightforward and falsifiable, but novelty is moderate as regularization is common; experiment easy, but penalty tuning may affect performance.

H003 (Representation drift) is highly novel and connects representation learning to bloat, but mechanism complex and experiment requires careful implementation of alignment loss, risking confounding results.
debate.synthesis: The debate highlights three potential causes of skill-library bloat: over-specialization from fine-grained tasks, lack of regularization, and representation drift. H001 is selected as the strongest candidate due to its clear mechanism, good falsifiability, and feasible experiment, while the other hypotheses offer complementary insights that could be integrated.
